test_id_1.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D, requiring members to use their Part D plan. However, NYRx does cover a specific, limited list of prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications. Pharmacies are required to bill Medicare first as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in a Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for a specific, limited list of drugs. Examples of these drugs include certain vitamins and cough and cold medications. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning it will only pay after all other reimbursement sources have been exhausted. Therefore, Medicare must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible enrollees. This includes potential third-party reimbursement sources.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Members and their prescribers must work to obtain coverage through the Part D plan or its exception and appeals process.

All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of the CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid generally does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that dual-eligible members must utilize their Medicare Part D plan for prescription drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both the provider manual and policy guidance confirm that federal law mandates Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The policy guidance explicitly lists examples of drugs covered by NYRx for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual mentions that members and prescribers must work together to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan or use its exception and appeals process, a detail not present in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid generally does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that dual-eligible members must utilize their Medicare Part D plan for prescription drug coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both the provider manual and policy guidance confirm that federal law mandates Medicaid as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first for dual-eligible claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The policy guidance explicitly lists examples of drugs covered by NYRx for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual mentions that members and prescribers must work together to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan or use its exception and appeals process, a detail not present in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_2.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, the NYS Medicaid program generally does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D, requiring members to use their Part D plan. However, NYRx does cover a specific, limited list of prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications. Pharmacies are required to bill Medicare first before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in a Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for a specific, limited list of drugs. This list includes certain vitamins and benzonatate. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning all other sources of reimbursement must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. This includes Medicare, which must be billed first for dual-eligible enrollees. Providers can refer to additional NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and utilize its coverage. This includes using the exception and appeals processes if necessary.

All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The policy explicitly lists specific drug categories, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications, that NYRx does cover for dual-eligible members, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and Medicare must be billed first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The policy explicitly lists specific drug categories, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications, that NYRx does cover for dual-eligible members, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

test_id_2.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_2.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_2.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_3.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D, requiring members to use their Part D plan. However, NYRx does cover a specific, limited list of prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications. Providers must bill Medicare first as Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in a Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for a specific list of drugs. This list includes certain vitamins and cough/cold medications. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning Medicaid will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs already covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plans exception and appeals process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions.

All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments must determine their business situation before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid does not cover drugs already covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill Medicare first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The policy guidance explicitly lists examples of drugs covered by NYRx for dual-eligible members, such as specific vitamins and cough/cold medications, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual details the requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services, including considerations for Medicare enrollment status, which is not as specifically outlined in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid does not cover drugs already covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to bill Medicare first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The policy guidance explicitly lists examples of drugs covered by NYRx for dual-eligible members, such as specific vitamins and cough/cold medications, which is not detailed in the provider manual [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual details the requirement for Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services, including considerations for Medicare enrollment status, which is not as specifically outlined in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_4.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D, requiring members to use their Part D plan or its exception process. However, NYRx does cover a specific, limited list of prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications, with all other prescription medications expected to be reimbursed through the Part D plan.
answers.policy_definition:
NYRx provides prescription drug coverage for dual-eligible members only for a specific list of drugs. This list includes certain vitamins and cough and cold medications. All other prescription medications for these members should be reimbursed through their Part D drug plan.

Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort. This means that Medicaid will only make payments after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid for dual-eligible enrollees.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and utilize it for coverage. This includes using the Part D plans exception and appeals process if necessary to obtain coverage for prescriptions not on the formulary.

All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of their CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing Medicare enrollments must determine if their business model requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible members, requiring other reimbursement sources like Medicare to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The provider manual states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, while the policy update specifies a limited list of drugs that NYRx does cover for these members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort for dual-eligible members, requiring other reimbursement sources like Medicare to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The provider manual states that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members, while the policy update specifies a limited list of drugs that NYRx does cover for these members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A complete list of CMS-Approved Accrediting Organizations may be found here:
www.cms.gov/files/document/accrediting-organization-contacts-prospective-clients.pdf
*The New York State (NYS) Medicaid program requires Medicare enrollment for pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid to
ensure that Medicaid dual eligible members receive drugs and supplies from Medicaid providers enrolled in
accordance with federal and State laws, and regulations. The three types of Medicare enrollment include CMS-460
Medicare Participating Physician or Supplier Agreement, CMS-855B Medicare enrollment for clinics/group practices
and other suppliers, and CMS-855S Medicare enrollment for durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and
supplies (DMEPOS) suppliers. Pharmacies that choose to enroll in the CMS-460 Medicare Participating Physician or
Supplier Agreement, agree to accept assignment for all their Medicare claims, including those not subject to Medicaid
payment. This Medicare enrollment is optional.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
However, all Medicaid enrolled pharmacy providers are still required by
federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare covered services provided to Medicaid dual eligible
members, regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status.
Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments, must determine if their business situation or business model
requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment or both before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
The Attestation of Need for/Exemption from Medicare Enrollment form #409602 located here:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/ProviderMaintForms/409602_Attestation_Need_Exemption_from_Me
dicare_Enrollment.pdf enables pharmacy applicants the opportunity to clarify and attest to the need for an exemption
from each type of Medicare enrollment. Note, all community pharmacy applicants would not qualify for exemption, see
table below.
See Table below, which describes pharmacy type with corresponding expected services and Medicare-type enrollment
expected at the time of pharmacy application submission. The more stringent Medicare requirement applies to mixed

pharmacy types.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_4.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_4.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_4.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_5.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for dual eligible pharmacy claims.

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid generally does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D, requiring members to use their Part D plan. However, NYRx does cover a specific, limited list of prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, such as certain vitamins and cough/cold medications. Pharmacies are required to bill Medicare first as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid members enrolled in a Medicare Part D or Medicare Advantage Drug Contracting plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for a specific list of drugs. These drugs include certain vitamins and cough and cold medications. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.

Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, meaning it will make payments only after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement sources, including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. This ensures that Medicaid only covers costs not covered by other payers.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan. Members and prescribers must use the Part D plans exception and appeals process if necessary.

All Medicaid-enrolled pharmacy providers are required by federal law to submit claims and accept assignments for Medicare-covered services provided to Medicaid dual-eligible members. This requirement applies regardless of CMS-460 enrollment status. Pharmacies missing one or more Medicare enrollments must determine if their business situation requires CMS-855B or CMS-855S Medicare enrollment before applying for NY Medicaid Pharmacy enrollment.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for most prescription drugs for dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both documents confirm that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].
differences: The policy guidance explicitly lists specific categories of drugs, such as certain vitamins and cough and cold medications, that NYRx does cover for dual-eligible members, which is not detailed in the provider manuals general statement [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual includes specific instructions for pharmacy providers regarding Medicare enrollment requirements (CMS-460, CMS-855B, or CMS-855S) for billing dual-eligible members, which is not present in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize that Medicare Part D is the primary payer for most prescription drugs for dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

Both documents confirm that Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort, requiring Medicare to be billed first for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1].

The policy guidance explicitly lists specific categories of drugs, such as certain vitamins and cough and cold medications, that NYRx does cover for dual-eligible members, which is not detailed in the provider manuals general statement [Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4].

The provider manual includes specific instructions for pharmacy providers regarding Medicare enrollment requirements (CMS-460, CMS-855B, or CMS-855S) for billing dual-eligible members, which is not present in the policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
2

Pharmacy Providers
Dual Eligible Coverage
For NYS Medicaid members who are enrolled in a Medicare Part D drug plan or Medicare Advantage Drug
Contracting (MAPD) plan, NYRx provides prescription drug coverage only for the drugs listed below. To
view all drugs covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible NYS Medicaid members, providers can refer to the NYS
Department of Health (DOH) “Drugs Covered by NYRx for Dual Eligible Members Effective 10/22/2020” web
page, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/medicare
_exempt_drugs.htm. All other prescription medications should be reimbursed through the Part D drug plan.
For the most up to date coverage information for the products below, providers should refer to the New York
State Department of Health List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs, located at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/fullform.pdf.
Prescription Drugs:
• Vitamins
Folic acid – oral
Hydroxocobalamin – injection
Vitamin B12 – injection/nasal
Vitamin D2 – oral
Vitamin K – oral
• Cough and Cold
• Benzonatate
Please note: NYRx does not provide dual eligible members with coverage of compounded
prescriptions.

process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

test_id_6.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_6.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible individuals, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources, including Medicare Part D, to be exhausted before Medicaid considers payment. Pharmacies must coordinate benefits and bill the appropriate primary payer first, accepting Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full without billing the patient for deductibles or coinsurance.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties, including other coverage, before billing Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for coordinating benefits and submitting claims to the proper primary payer first.

New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing New York State Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits and ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper coordination of benefits.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid members who are dual-eligible must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan. Medicaid does not cover drugs that are covered by Medicare Part D for these members. The total Medicare and Medicaid payment will not exceed the Medicaid-only fee, and providers cannot bill dual-eligible individuals for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance.

Providers must determine if a patient has other insurance and submit claims to that carrier before billing Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The amount paid by other insurance must be entered on the claim form. If the provider does not know if the service is covered, they must still submit a claim to the other insurance carrier first.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other insurance to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources state that providers must coordinate benefits and submit claims to the primary payer before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Medicare Part D for these members, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides specific instructions regarding not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee and not billing dual-eligible individuals for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance, which is more granular than the general policy statement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other insurance to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources state that providers must coordinate benefits and submit claims to the primary payer before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically details that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Medicare Part D for these members, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides specific instructions regarding not exceeding the Medicaid-only fee and not billing dual-eligible individuals for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance, which is more granular than the general policy statement [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_6.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_6.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_7.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_7.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible individuals, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources, including Medicare Part D, to be exhausted before Medicaid considers payment. Pharmacies must coordinate benefits and bill other liable third parties, such as Medicare Part D plans, prior to submitting claims to NYS Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers are required to bill applicable third parties and exhaust all existing benefits. This includes coordinating with the proper primary payer before billing New York State Medicaid.

New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing New York State Medicaid. New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage first.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. These members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan. Providers must utilize the Part D plans coverage or its exception and appeals process.

Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance and must submit a claim to that carrier prior to billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The amount paid by other insurance must be entered in the 'Other Insurance Paid' field.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to bill other insurance carriers or exhaust other benefits before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically mentions Medicare Part D as a primary payer for dual-eligible members, stating that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides a broader statement about billing applicable third parties without specifically naming Medicare Part D in the context of dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual details a specific field, 'Other Insurance Paid,' for entering amounts paid by other carriers when billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require providers to bill other insurance carriers or exhaust other benefits before submitting claims to Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically mentions Medicare Part D as a primary payer for dual-eligible members, stating that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update provides a broader statement about billing applicable third parties without specifically naming Medicare Part D in the context of dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual details a specific field, 'Other Insurance Paid,' for entering amounts paid by other carriers when billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines].
