test_id_8.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_8.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible individuals, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring all other available resources, including Medicare Part D, to be exhausted before Medicaid considers payment. Pharmacies must coordinate benefits and bill the appropriate primary payer first, accepting Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full without billing the patient for deductibles or coinsurance.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, requiring providers to bill applicable third parties and exhaust all existing benefits before billing Medicaid. Pharmacies are responsible for coordinating benefits and submitting claims to other coverage, including the proper primary payer, before submitting to New York State Medicaid.

New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing New York State Medicaid, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits and ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper coordination of benefits.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid members who are dual-eligible must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for these individuals. Providers cannot bill dual-eligible individuals for Medicare Part B deductibles or coinsurance, as the combined Medicare and Medicaid payment is considered payment in full.

Providers must determine if a patient has other insurance coverage and submit claims to that carrier before billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort. The amount paid by other insurance must be entered on the claim form.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other insurance benefits to be exhausted first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the providers responsibility to coordinate benefits with other payers before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically mentions that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for these individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly refers to "applicable third parties" and "other coverage" without explicitly naming Medicare Part D in the context of dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other insurance benefits to be exhausted first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the providers responsibility to coordinate benefits with other payers before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically mentions that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for these individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly refers to "applicable third parties" and "other coverage" without explicitly naming Medicare Part D in the context of dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• If date of notification of eligibility is after 90 days of DOS, LTC/FC facility pharmacies may send the claim
information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_8.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.

test_id_9.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_9.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible pharmacy claims, Medicare Part D is the primary payer, and Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Part D. Providers must bill other liable third parties, including Medicare, before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.
answers.policy_definition:
NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Pharmacies must coordinate with the proper primary payer and exhaust all existing benefits.

NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing NYS Medicaid.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For dual-eligible members, Medicare Part D is the primary payer for prescription drugs. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D. The combined Medicare and Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full, and providers cannot seek to recover Part B deductible or coinsurance from dual-eligible individuals.

Providers are responsible for determining if a patient has other insurance. Providers must submit a claim to other insurance carriers prior to billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require providers to bill other insurance carriers or third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically identifies Medicare Part D as the primary payer for prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, which is a more specific detail than the general policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require providers to bill other insurance carriers or third parties before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically identifies Medicare Part D as the primary payer for prescription drugs for dual-eligible members, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual specifies that Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, which is a more specific detail than the general policy guidance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacy Claims
For pharmacy claims, providers should refer to page 15 of the eMedNY New York State Billing Guidelines –
Pharmacy, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Billing_Guide
lines.pdf. Per page 39 of the eMedNY Prospective Drug Utilization Review/Electronic Claims Capture and
Adjudication ProDUR/ECCA Provider Manual, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/
Pharmacy/ProDUR-D.0-ECCA_Provider_Manual/Pro%20DUR%20ECCA%20Provider%20Manual%20
(D.0).pdf, “when filling prescriptions/orders for a member who is restricted to a primary provider (physician,
clinic, podiatrist, or dentist) and the primary provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the NPI of this
provider must be entered in the ordering/prescribing field.”
Additionally, “if the restricted member was referred by the primary provider to another provider and the
referred-to provider is the prescribing/ordering provider, the pharmacy provider must enter the referred-to
provider’s NPI in the ordering/prescribing field. The primary provider’s NPI must be entered as the
referring/primary care provider.”
April 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg.

information for retro-PA consideration, within 30 days from the date of notification of eligibility, to the
Medicaid Pharmacy unit at PPNO@health.ny.gov. All claims submitted for PA after 30 days of
eligibility determination will be denied. Additional information on submitting claims over 90 from DOS
June 2019 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/QuickRefDocs/FOD-7001_Sub_Claims_Over_90_
days_Old.pdf.
• Any claim submitted with a DOS after the date of notification of eligibility will be denied. The claim should
have been billed on the DOS.
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility as well as the pharmacy, to actively and
regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their members.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, which may include the facility,
commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Additional information regarding timely billing can be found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2019 Volume 35 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluate medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_10.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, New York State Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance both state that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B or Part C deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from these individuals. Providers are required to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full.
answers.policy_definition:
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service.

The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid. This implies that if Medicaid does not cover the co-payment, the member is not responsible for it.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. This is in accordance with state regulation.

Providers cannot proactively waive co-pays. Co-pays may be waived only on a case-by-case basis. This waiver is permissible due to financial need and must be at the request of the individual member or enrollee.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from collecting certain co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents emphasize that providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits for pharmacy providers, while the policy update focuses on Medicare Part C benefits for providers generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual explicitly states that co-pays cannot be proactively waived and outlines conditions for case-by-case waivers based on financial need, whereas the policy update implies a non-collection of co-payments for dual-eligible members when not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid without detailing waiver conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from collecting certain co-payment or coinsurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents emphasize that providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits for pharmacy providers, while the policy update focuses on Medicare Part C benefits for providers generally [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual explicitly states that co-pays cannot be proactively waived and outlines conditions for case-by-case waivers based on financial need, whereas the policy update implies a non-collection of co-payments for dual-eligible members when not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid without detailing waiver conditions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_12.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, New York State Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance both state that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B or Part C deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from these individuals. Providers are required to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full. The provider manual further clarifies that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition:
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. This is in accordance with state regulation.

Providers cannot proactively waive co-pays. Co-pays may only be waived on a case-by-case basis. This waiver is granted based on financial need and by request of the individual member or enrollee.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from billing dually eligible individuals for certain Medicare co-payment or coinsurance amounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents emphasize that providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits and the inability to recover deductible or coinsurance amounts from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance focuses on Medicare Part C benefits, stating that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual includes general rules regarding the proactive waiver of co-pays for Medicaid members based on financial need [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance does not provide general rules for co-pay waivers but rather a specific instance related to Medicare Part C co-payments for dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_12.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_12.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_12.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_13.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance both state that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B or Part C deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from these individuals. Providers must accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full. The provider manual further clarifies that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
answers.provider_manual_definition: A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. Additionally, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that providers cannot seek to recover deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits and the associated deductibles or coinsurance, while the policy focuses on Medicare Part C benefits and co-payments or co-insurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual includes a clarification that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, which is not present in the policy document [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that providers cannot seek to recover deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits and the associated deductibles or coinsurance, while the policy focuses on Medicare Part C benefits and co-payments or co-insurance [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual includes a clarification that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, which is not present in the policy document [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.

• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and

• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.

• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,

• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

test_id_14.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, the provider manual states that pharmacy providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts, and Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D. Policy guidance reinforces that providers cannot recover Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amounts from dual-eligible individuals, requiring acceptance of Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full.
answers.policy_definition:
A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.

Policy guidance broadly states that dual-eligible individuals are subject to Medicare rules. This implicitly impacts co-payment responsibilities. However, it does not detail specific conditions for co-payment waivers.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Pharmacy providers of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. Additionally, Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Medicare and Medicaid payments must be accepted as payment in full.

Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only on a case-by-case basis due to financial need. This waiver must be upon the request of the individual member or enrollee.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts from dual-eligible members for Medicare-covered benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance amounts, and states that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy guidance focuses on Medicare Part C co-payment and co-insurance amounts, prohibiting providers from billing dual-eligible individuals for any unreimbursed amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual explicitly details the conditions under which co-payments can be waived, requiring a case-by-case basis due to financial need and upon member request [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy guidance broadly states that dual-eligible individuals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not provide specific details on co-payment waiver conditions [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize that providers cannot recover co-payment or co-insurance amounts from dual-eligible members for Medicare-covered benefits [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both sources require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B deductible and coinsurance amounts, and states that Medicaid does not cover drugs under Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy guidance focuses on Medicare Part C co-payment and co-insurance amounts, prohibiting providers from billing dual-eligible individuals for any unreimbursed amounts [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

The provider manual explicitly details the conditions under which co-payments can be waived, requiring a case-by-case basis due to financial need and upon member request [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

Policy guidance broadly states that dual-eligible individuals are subject to Medicare rules, but it does not provide specific details on co-payment waiver conditions [New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7].

test_id_14.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_14.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_14.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

test_id_15.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

answers.headline_summary: For dual-eligible members, New York State Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance both state that providers cannot seek to recover Medicare Part B or Part C deductible, coinsurance, or co-payment amounts directly from these individuals. Providers are required to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dual-eligible members.
answers.policy_definition: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment or co-insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
answers.provider_manual_definition: A pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals. The Medicare and Medicaid payment, if any, must be accepted as payment in full. This is in accordance with state regulation.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from seeking to recover cost-sharing amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits and pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy guidance specifically addresses Medicare Part C benefits and providers in general [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance prohibit providers from seeking to recover cost-sharing amounts directly from dually eligible individuals [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

Both documents require providers to accept Medicare and Medicaid payments as payment in full for services rendered to dually eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].
The provider manual specifically addresses Medicare Part B benefits and pharmacy providers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
The policy guidance specifically addresses Medicare Part C benefits and providers in general [New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5].

policy.text:
Pharmacies will only be able to bill for Medicaid non-dual eligible members. Dual eligible members will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare. Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through their health plans. For Medicaid Managed Care
Organization (MCO) billing guidance, providers must contact the individual plan. Providers can access
individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page at:
https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2025 Volume 41 Number 7
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Dual-eligible enrollees will
continue to access immunization services through Medicare.
• Medicaid managed care (MMC) members will continue to access immunization services through their
health plans. For Medicaid managed care organization (MCO) billing guidance, please contact the MCO.
• Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient-specific order or non-patient-specific
order. These orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber’s National Provider
Identification (NPI) is required on the claim for the claim to be paid.
• Vaccines recommended by the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) for individuals
younger than 19 years of age are provided to Medicaid members, both fee-for-service (FFS) and MMC,
free of charge by the Vaccines for Children (VFC) program.
Pharmacies wishing to administer VFC-available vaccines to Medicaid members younger than 19
years of age may enroll in the VFC program. Note: The VFC program is currently enrolling
pharmacies to receive influenza vaccine only.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
• It is the responsibility of the provider to submit the claim using the appropriate claim format with their
usual or customary fee indicated along with reporting the Medicare Advantage Plan deductible
amount, co-insurance amount, co-payment amount and paid amount.
Please note: A provider of a Medicare Part C benefit cannot attempt to recover any co-payment or co-
insurance amount directly from Medicare and NYS Medicaid dually eligible individuals. The provider is
required to accept the Medicare Part C health plan payment and any NYS Medicaid payment as payment in
full for the service. The NYS Medicaid member may not be billed for any Medicare Part C co-payment/co-
insurance amount that is not reimbursed by NYS Medicaid.
May 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medical and Surgical Supplies
Reimbursement for each covered medical/surgical supply will be the lower of:
• The price as indicated on the New York State List of Medical/Surgical Supplies, or
• The usual and customary price charged to the general public.
"Covered supplies" are those on the OTC and Supply Fee Schedule manual located here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/index.aspx. For supplies not on this list, only those supplies for
which the prescriber has obtained prior approval are covered.
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Medicaid and provider manual guidance align on the principle that Medicaid will cover certain drug classes specifically excluded from Medicare Part D, such as select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. For dual-eligible members, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for these excluded drugs, provided claims are correctly submitted and coordinated with other payers.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from third-party liability plans, including Medicare. This coordination of benefits ensures payment for excluded drug classes. A pharmacy provider receiving a zero-fill response from a third-party liability plan must ensure the claim was adjudicated within the plans claim process requirements.

When a member has Medicare, providers should bill Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs or Medicare Part D/MAPD for other prescription drugs. If a member has Medicare Parts A or B but is not enrolled in Part D, providers should bill the LINET program. This initiates Part D enrollment and facilitates payment for Part D covered claims.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only covers drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans. This includes select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs for Medicare Part D members. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

Medicare members who are also dual-eligible for Medicaid must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any drug class that is already covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work with the Part D plan to find appropriate drug coverage or use the plans exception and appeals process.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid will cover drug classes specifically excluded from Medicare Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources acknowledge the importance of coordinating benefits for dual-eligible members to ensure appropriate coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid does not cover any drug class already covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, emphasizing the need for members to work with their Part D plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy provides specific instructions for billing Medicare Part B or Part D, and for utilizing the LINET program for members not enrolled in Part D, which is not detailed in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid will cover drug classes specifically excluded from Medicare Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources acknowledge the importance of coordinating benefits for dual-eligible members to ensure appropriate coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual explicitly states that Medicaid does not cover any drug class already covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members, emphasizing the need for members to work with their Part D plan [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy provides specific instructions for billing Medicare Part B or Part D, and for utilizing the LINET program for members not enrolled in Part D, which is not detailed in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

policy.text:
Most items do not require additional authorization prior to dispensing and billing. These items
may be dispensed and billed directly to Medicaid.
• The items in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics, Orthotics,
Supplies – Procedure Codes and Coverage Guidelines document, found at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/DME_Procedure_Codes.pdf, are not subject
to the carve-out and should continue to be billed to the member’s MC plan.
• There are some DMEPOS items subject to the carve-out, that require pre-approval. To ensure a
smooth transition for Medicaid MC member’s, PA requirements will be temporarily relaxed (e.g.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
