provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_16.ground_truth:
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_16.pm_ground_truth: Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.

This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_17.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Medicaid and provider manual guidance align on the principle that Medicaid generally does not cover drugs for Medicare Part D members, except for specific drug classes legally excluded from Part D coverage. Both sources emphasize that for dual-eligible members, Medicaid will only pay for these Part D-excluded drugs, subject to Medicaid rules including prior authorization.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under third-party liability (TPL) plans, including Medicare. This is done through coordination of benefits. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response from a TPL plan must ensure the claim was adjudicated within each TPL plans claim process requirements.

When a member has Medicare, providers will bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C) for prescription drugs. If the primary insurer, including Part D, does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Any claim issues, including prior authorization requirements, must be resolved with the third party before submitting the claim to Medicaid.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and prescribers must work with the Part D plan to find covered drugs. Alternatively, they can use the plan's exception and appeals process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan's formulary.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid will cover drugs for dual-eligible members only if the drug class is legally excluded from Medicare Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid generally does not cover drugs that are covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly mentions that all claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization, for Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance provides more detail on the process for submitting claims to Medicaid after a zero-fill reimbursement from a third-party liability plan, emphasizing the need to ensure adjudication within the TPL plans claim process requirements [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance state that Medicaid will cover drugs for dual-eligible members only if the drug class is legally excluded from Medicare Part D coverage [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid generally does not cover drugs that are covered by Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual explicitly mentions that all claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization, for Part D-excluded drugs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy guidance provides more detail on the process for submitting claims to Medicaid after a zero-fill reimbursement from a third-party liability plan, emphasizing the need to ensure adjudication within the TPL plans claim process requirements [New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Information
regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the
Provider Manuals at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/index.aspx within the “Procedure
Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2020 Volume 36 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_18.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance differ in handling Medicare Part D-excluded drugs?

answers.headline_summary: Medicaid and provider manual guidance align on the principle that Medicaid covers drugs specifically excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members. Both sources specify that Medicaid will only pay for certain drug classes, such as select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs, that are not covered by Part.
answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being covered under third-party liability (TPL) Plans, including Medicare. This payment occurs through coordination of benefits. Medicaid will also pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claims.

When a member has Medicare, providers will bill Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C) for prescription drugs. Providers must resolve any claim issues, including prior authorization, with the third party before submitting to Medicaid. If the primary insurer does not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under Part D plans for Medicare Part D members. Examples of such drugs include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work with the Part D plan for coverage or use its exception and appeals process. Dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid covers drugs specifically excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer for dual-eligible members, covering specific excluded categories or patient responsibility after Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
differences: The provider manual explicitly lists select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs as examples of Part D-excluded drugs covered by Medicaid, while the policy update refers more broadly to "drugs in classes specifically excluded" without providing specific examples [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual emphasizes that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid covers drugs specifically excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources indicate that Medicaid acts as a secondary payer for dual-eligible members, covering specific excluded categories or patient responsibility after Medicare Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

The provider manual explicitly lists select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs as examples of Part D-excluded drugs covered by Medicaid, while the policy update refers more broadly to "drugs in classes specifically excluded" without providing specific examples [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual emphasizes that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

answers.policy_definition:
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from third-party liability plans, including Medicare. This coordination of benefits ensures appropriate payment for these excluded drug classes.

When a member has Medicare, providers should bill Medicare Part B for Part B-covered drugs or Medicare Part D/MAPD for other prescription drugs. If a member has Medicare Parts A or B but is not enrolled in Part D, providers should bill the LINET program to initiate Part D enrollment and facilitate claim payment.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Medicaid only covers drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs when the drug class is specifically excluded by law from Medicare Part D plans. Examples include select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs. All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

Medicare members who are also Medicaid-eligible, known as dual-eligible individuals, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs that are already covered under Medicare Part D for these full-benefit dual-eligible members. Members and prescribers must collaborate to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan or utilize the Part D plans exception and appeals process.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance confirm that Medicaid will cover drug classes specifically excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources emphasize the importance of proper claim submission and coordination of benefits for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].
differences: The provider manual explicitly lists select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs as examples of Part D-excluded drug classes covered by Medicaid, while the policy guidance refers more broadly to 'drugs in classes specifically excluded' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual focuses on what Medicaid does not cover for dual-eligible members under Medicare Part D, whereas the policy guidance details the billing hierarchy for providers when a member has Medicare, including billing Medicare Part B or Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance confirm that Medicaid will cover drug classes specifically excluded from Medicare Part D for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

Both sources emphasize the importance of proper claim submission and coordination of benefits for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual explicitly lists select prescription vitamins and certain non-prescription drugs as examples of Part D-excluded drug classes covered by Medicaid, while the policy guidance refers more broadly to 'drugs in classes specifically excluded' [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13].

The provider manual focuses on what Medicaid does not cover for dual-eligible members under Medicare Part D, whereas the policy guidance details the billing hierarchy for providers when a member has Medicare, including billing Medicare Part B or Part D [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12].

policy.text:
Through
coordination of benefits, Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid coverable
claims or will pay up to the Medicaid allowed amounts for drugs in classes specifically excluded from being
covered under the third-party liability (TPL) Plan, including Medicare.
As previously communicated in the December 2020 issue of the Medicaid Update, in the article titled
Submission Guidance for Fee-for-Service Claims with Third Party Liability (Medicare or Other Insurance),
available at https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2020/docs/mu_no18_dec20
_pr.pdf, a claim submitted to NYS Medicaid with a zero-fill reimbursement from any TPL Plan must be
submitted. A pharmacy provider who receives a zero-fill response must ensure that the claim was adjudicated
within each TPL Plan claim process requirement. This includes submitting a claim under the appropriate
coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System
(HCPCS) or by National Drug Code (NDC).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update November 2021 Volume 37 Number 13
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.

regarding Medicaid fee-for-service covered “Practitioner Administered Drugs” can be found in the

Code and Fee Schedule” sections for Drugs, and in “Provider Communications”.
• For coverage information regarding Practitioner Administered Drugs for managed care enrollees,
please refer to the managed care plan's medical benefit of policies and procedures.
• Questions should be directed to the eMedNY Call Center at (800) 343-9000.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The
Medicaid Program does not provide reimbursement for replacement supplies of lost, stolen, or misdirected
medication, medical/surgical supply or DME deliveries or when confirmation of delivery was made with
delivery industry tracking receipts (where there is no handwritten or electronic signature of receipt by
recipient or authorized agent or designee).
• Once delivered and signed for by a facility or agent of a facility, the site of receipt/handling/administration
is responsible for replacement of improperly stored, handled, lost, or stolen drugs.
• The pharmacy must ensure proper storage is available and authorized agent or recipient is aware of
requirements before delivery.

• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
• The Department reserves the right to require member or caregiver signatures in situations where there is
high probability of lost or stolen items, or an increase in complaints from members concerning lost or
stolen items.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_21.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort. This requirement applies to dually eligible members, and pharmacies are responsible for ensuring all other available benefits are exhausted prior to submitting claims to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, that may be liable for a claim before billing New York State Medicaid. New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.

Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to New York State Medicaid. Pharmacies are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer.

Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacies must not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members.

Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage according to the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Submitting a claim that bypasses the third-party payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing.

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, known as dual-eligible individuals, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other applicable coverage, such as Medicare, to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that pharmacies are responsible for coordinating benefits and submitting claims to other coverage before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part D for these members, a detail not directly addressed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update mentions the Medicare enrollment requirement for fee-for-service pharmacies, which is not explicitly detailed in the provider manuals discussion of Medicare Part D coverage for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires other applicable coverage, such as Medicare, to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that pharmacies are responsible for coordinating benefits and submitting claims to other coverage before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual explicitly states that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan and that Medicaid does not cover drugs covered by Part D for these members, a detail not directly addressed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update mentions the Medicare enrollment requirement for fee-for-service pharmacies, which is not explicitly detailed in the provider manuals discussion of Medicare Part D coverage for dual-eligible members [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_21.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_21.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_21.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_22.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_22.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid acts as the payer of last resort. This requirement applies to dually eligible members, and pharmacies are responsible for ensuring all other available benefits are exhausted prior to submitting claims to Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, that may be liable for a claim before billing New York State Medicaid. New York State Medicaid is always the payer of last resort.

Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to New York State Medicaid. Pharmacies are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer.

Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare, and pharmacies can only bill Medicaid after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacies must not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members.

Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Submitting a claim that bypasses the third-party payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing.

Medicare members who also have Medicaid, known as dual-eligible individuals, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D prescription drug plan. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual-eligible members.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other applicable coverage, such as Medicare, to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacys responsibility to coordinate benefits and submit claims to other coverage before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid will not cover drugs covered by Part D for these members, a detail not directly addressed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies Medicare enrollment requirements for fee-for-service pharmacies versus Managed Care Only pharmacies, which is not detailed in the provider manuals discussion of billing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort, requiring other applicable coverage, such as Medicare, to be billed first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacys responsibility to coordinate benefits and submit claims to other coverage before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual explicitly states that dual-eligible members must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan and that Medicaid will not cover drugs covered by Part D for these members, a detail not directly addressed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update specifies Medicare enrollment requirements for fee-for-service pharmacies versus Managed Care Only pharmacies, which is not detailed in the provider manuals discussion of billing order [New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].

answers.provider_manual_definition:
Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacies must not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members.

Pharmacies have a responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage according to the plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Submitting a claim that bypasses the third-party payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require pharmacies to bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacys responsibility to coordinate benefits and exhaust other coverage options before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that circumventing Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members is not allowed, providing a specific operational detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual specifies that submitting a claim that bypasses a third-party payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing and is subject to audit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing New York State Medicaid, while the provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy responsibilities [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update state that Medicaid is the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents require pharmacies to bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources emphasize the pharmacys responsibility to coordinate benefits and exhaust other coverage options before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual explicitly states that circumventing Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members is not allowed, providing a specific operational detail [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual specifies that submitting a claim that bypasses a third-party payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing and is subject to audit [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing New York State Medicaid, while the provider manual focuses specifically on pharmacy responsibilities [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
with Other Payer ID and Other Payer ID Qualifier for Pharmacy
Claims
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of last resort and federal regulations
require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2024 Volume 40 Number 5
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacies may enroll in Medicaid as a billing provider (also known as a fee-for-service (FFS) provider) or as a
Managed Care Only (non-billing) provider. Fee-for-service pharmacies require Medicare enrollment. Managed
Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. NYS Medicaid does not enroll out of state,
mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care (LTC), or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers
unless the NYS Department of Health (DOH) determines an unmet need for such pharmacy enrollment.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.

Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Otherwise, leave this field blank.
Other Insurance Paid (Field 24)
This field must be completed if the member is covered by insurance other than Medicare.
When applicable, enter the amount paid by the other insurance carrier in this field.
Leave this field blank if the member has no other insurance coverage.

NOTE: It is the responsibility of the provider to determine whether the patient is covered by other insurance and
whether the insurance carrier covers the service being billed for. If the service is covered or if the provider does not
know if the service is covered, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid,
as Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
Pharmacy Claim Form-Certification Section: Fields 29 to 31
Signature (Field 29)
The provider or an authorized representative must sign the claim form.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Billing Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_23.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_23.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_23.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_24.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_24.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program are required to bill Medicare first for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid is the payer of last resort. This includes maximizing Medicare benefits, particularly for dual-eligible members who must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, before billing New York State Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations mandate that all other available resources be used first.

Through coordination of benefits, New York State Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted New York State Medicaid coverable claims, up to the allowed amount. This payment occurs only after providers exhaust all existing benefits. Providers should always ask a New York State Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage.

Fee-for-service pharmacies enrolling in Medicaid are required to also enroll in Medicare. Managed Care Only enrolled pharmacies are not required to enroll in Medicare. New York State Medicaid does not enroll out-of-state, mail order, compounding, infusion, Long Term Care, or specialty pharmacies as FFS billing providers unless an unmet need is determined.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program must participate in Medicare and maximize Medicare benefits before billing Medicaid. Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacies cannot circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare-covered member.

Submitting a claim that bypasses the Third-Party Liability (TPL) payers responsibility is considered fraudulent billing and is subject to audit. Providers must retain evidence of TPL denial or payment information for a minimum of six years. This ensures proper adjudication before resubmitting to Medicaid.

Medicare members who are also Medicaid dual-eligible must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D plan to maintain Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under Medicare Part D for these members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an appropriate drug covered by the Part D plan.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid for services covered by both programs, emphasizing Medicaids role as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources highlight the importance of proper coordination of benefits and the necessity of exhausting other benefits before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual explicitly states that bypassing Third-Party Liability payers is considered fraudulent billing and is subject to audit, a detail not directly mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides specific guidance on dual-eligible members needing to enroll in a Medicare Part D plan and Medicaid not covering Part D drugs for these members, while the policy update focuses on general Medicare enrollment requirements for fee-for-service pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy state that pharmacies must bill Medicare before Medicaid for services covered by both programs, emphasizing Medicaids role as the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources highlight the importance of proper coordination of benefits and the necessity of exhausting other benefits before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual explicitly states that bypassing Third-Party Liability payers is considered fraudulent billing and is subject to audit, a detail not directly mentioned in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides specific guidance on dual-eligible members needing to enroll in a Medicare Part D plan and Medicaid not covering Part D drugs for these members, while the policy update focuses on general Medicare enrollment requirements for fee-for-service pharmacies [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3].

Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_24.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

test_id_25.question: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual rules for when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare.
test_id_25.concept: when a pharmacy must bill Medicaid before billing Medicare

answers.headline_summary: Pharmacies must bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. This applies to dually eligible members, and pharmacies are responsible for coordinating benefits and exhausting all other available resources.
answers.policy_definition:
New York State Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties, including Medicare, before billing New York State Medicaid. Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing New York State Medicaid.

Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to New York State Medicaid. Pharmacies are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting claims under the appropriate coverage benefit, whether medical or pharmacy.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Services covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare. Pharmacies can only bill Medicaid after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacies must not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members.

It is the providers responsibility to determine if a patient has other insurance and if the service is covered. If so, the provider must submit a claim to the other insurance carrier prior to billing Medicaid. Medicaid is the payer of last resort.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is always the payer of last resort [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both sources require pharmacies to bill Medicare before billing Medicaid for services covered by both programs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that providers must exhaust all other available resources or existing benefits before billing Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically instructs pharmacies not to circumvent Medicare billing procedures for Medicare-covered members, a detail not explicitly stated in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update explicitly mentions that pharmacies are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer, including submitting claims under the appropriate coverage benefit (medical or pharmacy), which is a more detailed instruction than found in the provider manual [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
