policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_32.ground_truth:
"It is the pharmacy's duty to make ""reasonable efforts"" to determine if a member has other primary insurance. It suggests asking the member or checking their eligibility information. The guidance is general and focuses on the principle

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission

The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP ""Other Payer"" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail."

test_id_34.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid requires providers to coordinate benefits by billing all other liable third parties before submitting claims to Medicaid, which acts as the payer of last resort. This includes pursuing coverage from sources like Workers' Compensation and other health insurance providers, with Medicaid covering patient responsibility up to the allowed amount for correctly submitted claims.
answers.policy_definition:
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits before billing NYS Medicaid. They should ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper Coordination of Benefits. Pharmacies are responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting them to NYS Medicaid.

NYS Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid, as Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations necessitate using all other available resources first. Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For work-related injuries, Workers' Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program if an open case exists. If a drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer. This requires using an Eligibility Clarification Code of '2' in field 309-C9.

Federal regulations and 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate that providers pursue any available other coverage before submitting a claim to Medicaid. Medicaid functions as the payer of last resort. Medicaid will cover the patient responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims or up to the Medicaid allowed amount for drugs excluded from the Third-Party Liability Plan.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that Medicaid is the payer of last resort and requires providers to exhaust all other available resources first [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

Both documents state that Medicaid will cover the patients responsibility for correctly submitted Medicaid-coverable claims, up to the Medicaid allowed amount [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically details the process for Workers' Compensation cases, including when NYRx can be billed as primary with a specific eligibility clarification code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers should ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper Coordination of Benefits, without specifying particular types of coverage like Workers' Compensation in that section [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically details the process for Workers' Compensation cases, including when NYRx can be billed as primary with a specific eligibility clarification code [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that providers should ask members about other third-party coverage to ensure proper Coordination of Benefits, without specifying particular types of coverage like Workers' Compensation in that section [New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

policy.text:
April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7

Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing
Instruction for New York State Medicaid
New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a
claim before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort. Federal regulations require
that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment. Through coordination of
benefits (COB), NYS Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for correctly submitted NYS Medicaid
coverable claims, up to the NYS Medicaid allowed amount.
Providers must exhaust all existing benefits prior to billing the NYS Medicaid. Providers should always ask a
NYS Medicaid member if they have other third-party coverage to ensure the proper COB. Pharmacies are
responsible for submitting claims to other coverage before submitting claims to NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies
are required to coordinate with the proper primary payer. This includes submitting a claim under the
appropriate coverage benefit, medical or pharmacy.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

• Attention Pharmacy Providers: Coordination of Benefits (COB) Processing Change for Other Payer

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Four: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Coordination of Benefits (COB) ensures the correct party pays first. NYS Medicaid is always the payor of
last resort; federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid
considers payment. If there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the health benefits of the
patient such as a health insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays
the lesser of Patient Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount. For
pharmacies, this rule applies to all PR, which includes deductible, co-insurance, copay, and other patient
responsibility.
Effective November 1, 2022, NYS Medicaid will not accept a combination of Other Coverage Code of
"3" in National Council for Prescription Drug Program (NCPDP) field 308-C8 (Other Coverage Code)
with any reject code in field 472-6E when another third party is responsible for payment. The pre-
adjudication edit will be expanded for this field and will return the NCPDP Reject Code (DE 3988) (6E - M/I
Other Payer Reject Code).
The provider must work with the primary insurance to obtain coverage for the member.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 5

Update to New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits Submission
NYRx, the New York State (NYS) Medicaid Pharmacy program, needs to ensure accurate Coordination of
Benefits (COB) submissions are recognized and other patient responsibility amounts are accepted. This is an
update to previous guidance issued in the Update on Medicaid Fee-for-Service (FFS) Pharmacy Billing
Instructions for Coordination of Benefits (COB) Submission article published in the July 2017 issue of the
Medicaid Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2017/jul17_
mu.pdf.
Federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. If
there is a responsible third-party that should be paying for the patients' health benefits, such as a health
insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Maintain inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies (e.g., diabetic), that are applicable to
the patients they serve.
• Maintain adequate hours of operation, as needed for the Medicaid members that they serve.
• Are able to respond to urgent or emergent issues that occur after normal operating hours.
• Out of state pharmacies:
o must maintain NY non-resident registration.
o must maintain state registration in service location.
o must maintain Pharmacy / Wholesale Distributor / Manufacturer License Verification with the NYS
Education Department.
o must maintain Medicaid enrollment in service location state.
• Update the Department as required for pharmacy enrollment changes as per the General Policy Guidelines.
• Maintain all required accreditations from a CMS-approved organization in compliance with CMS
regulations.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_35.question: What are the requirements for Coordination of Benefits (COB)?

answers.headline_summary: Coordination of Benefits (COB) requires that all available resources be used before NYRx Medicaid considers payment. Providers must bill applicable third parties before submitting claims to NYRx Medicaid.
answers.policy_definition:
The April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update reminds providers of the requirement to bill applicable third parties before billing NYS Medicaid. NYS Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations mandate the use of all other available resources first.

Additional information regarding Coordination of Benefits (COB) can be found in various Medicaid Update articles. These articles provide guidance on COB submissions for Medicaid Fee-for-Service Pharmacy Billing and emphasize the requirement for Medicaid to be the payer of last resort.

The May 2023 New York State Medicaid Update provides an update to billing instructions for Coordination of Benefits submission for NYRx, the New York State Medicaid Pharmacy program. It reiterates that federal regulations require all other available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If a member has a work-related injury with an open Workers' Compensation case, Workers' Compensation coverage must be used before billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program. However, if the drug claim is not related to a Workers' Compensation case, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of "2" in field 309-C9.

Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) mandate that a provider pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party, they must pay first.

Pharmacies are required to maintain an inventory of commonly dispensed medications and supplies, such as diabetic supplies, that are applicable to the patients they serve. They must also maintain adequate hours of operation for Medicaid members.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy updates emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses Workers' Compensation cases and provides guidance on billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program for work-related versus unrelated drug claims, which is not detailed in the provided policy updates [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy updates emphasize that federal regulations require all available resources to be used before Medicaid considers payment [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4].

The provider manual specifically addresses Workers' Compensation cases and provides guidance on billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program for work-related versus unrelated drug claims, which is not detailed in the provided policy updates [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The manual requires that when Medicaid is billed as a secondary payer, the claim must indicate the amount paid by the primary insurer. It lacks the specific coding instructions needed for an electronic claim submission
test_id_35.policy_ground_truth: The claim to Medicaid must include the correct NCPDP "Other Payer" fields, including codes indicating the primary payer type, the amount they paid, and the member's remaining responsibility. This is a critical operational clarification; without these codes, the claim would fail.

test_id_36.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_36.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying the legitimacy of the prescription and the identity of the prescriber. They may fill prescriptions if the missing patient information is readily available in pharmacy records or if they are otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate.
answers.policy_definition:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use. They should also consult the prescriber, as necessary, regarding any changes.

When a non-preferred drug prescription requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must inform the prescriber that PA is needed and notify the patient once the prescriber obtains it. If the prescriber cannot be reached, the pharmacist may only initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply. Generally, pharmacists are not authorized to submit PAs or enter agreements with prescribers for PAs, except for the 72-hour emergency supply or if employed by the prescriber.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If a Medicaid members address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) is missing from a prescription, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must adhere to Public Health Law requirements, and pharmacists can only add or change specific information.

The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescribers identity. This is particularly important if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize the pharmacists role in ensuring prescription accuracy and legitimacy, either by verifying patient information or consulting the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifically addresses the pharmacists discretion regarding missing patient demographic information on prescriptions, allowing them to proceed if information is readily available or the prescription is legitimate, whereas the policy guidance focuses on verifying prescription directions and consulting the prescriber for changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual outlines the pharmacists responsibility to verify the prescribers identity and prescription validity, while the policy guidance details the pharmacists role in prior authorization for non-preferred drugs, including communication with the prescriber and patient, and limitations on initiating PAs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy guidance emphasize the pharmacists role in ensuring prescription accuracy and legitimacy, either by verifying patient information or consulting the prescriber [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual specifically addresses the pharmacists discretion regarding missing patient demographic information on prescriptions, allowing them to proceed if information is readily available or the prescription is legitimate, whereas the policy guidance focuses on verifying prescription directions and consulting the prescriber for changes [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual outlines the pharmacists responsibility to verify the prescribers identity and prescription validity, while the policy guidance details the pharmacists role in prior authorization for non-preferred drugs, including communication with the prescriber and patient, and limitations on initiating PAs [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_36.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

test_id_37.question: What responsibilities do pharmacists have when required patient or prescriber information is missing?
test_id_37.concept: pharmacist responsibilities when required patient or prescriber information is missing

answers.headline_summary: When required patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying prescription legitimacy and prescriber identity. For missing patient details, pharmacists may fill prescriptions if the information is readily available or if they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate.
answers.policy_definition:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary. This consultation is important for any changes that may be required.

Pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists have a corresponding responsibility to ensure that recognized standards of pharmacy practice are maintained. They must also ensure that prescription claims submitted for payment are true, accurate, and for a legitimate medical purpose.

When a prescription for a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber that PA is needed and inform the patient once the prescriber obtains it. If the pharmacist cannot reach the prescriber and no PA is obtained, the pharmacist may only initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit PAs or enter agreements with prescribers for PAs, with exceptions for the 72-hour emergency supply or when employed by a prescriber.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If a Medicaid members address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) is missing from a prescription, the pharmacist is not required to enter this information. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must adhere to Public Health Law requirements, which permit pharmacists to add or change only specific information.

The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the identity of the prescriber if the prescriber is unknown to them. Electronic imaging of prescriptions must be an exact reproduction of the original order. Such reproductions may also require authentication.

All prescriptions and fiscal orders must include the patient's name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN). If the CIN is not present on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the pharmacy's records.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the importance of accurate prescription information for proper dispensing and claims processing.
Both documents highlight the pharmacists role in ensuring the legitimacy of prescriptions and adherence to regulatory standards.
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance on how pharmacists should handle missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, detailing conditions under which a prescription can still be filled [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update offers detailed instructions for pharmacists regarding prior authorization for non-preferred drugs, including specific notification requirements and limitations on initiating PAs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual focuses on the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity and prescription validity through good faith efforts, especially for unknown prescribers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists are responsible for maintaining pharmacy practice standards and ensuring claims are for legitimate medical purposes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the importance of accurate prescription information for proper dispensing and claims processing.
Both documents highlight the pharmacists role in ensuring the legitimacy of prescriptions and adherence to regulatory standards.

The provider manual provides specific guidance on how pharmacists should handle missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, detailing conditions under which a prescription can still be filled [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update offers detailed instructions for pharmacists regarding prior authorization for non-preferred drugs, including specific notification requirements and limitations on initiating PAs [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual focuses on the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity and prescription validity through good faith efforts, especially for unknown prescribers [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update broadly states that pharmacy owners and supervising pharmacists are responsible for maintaining pharmacy practice standards and ensuring claims are for legitimate medical purposes [New York State Medicaid Update April 2023 Volume 39 Number 8].

When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug requiring prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber that PA is required and inform the patient once the prescriber obtains it. If the prescriber cannot be reached and no PA is obtained, the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit PAs or enter agreements for PAs on behalf of prescribers, except for emergency supplies or when employed by a prescriber.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
If the address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) of a Medicaid member are missing from a prescription, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items if the information is readily available in pharmacy records and the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or if the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Controlled substance prescriptions must adhere to Article 33 of the Public Health Law, and pharmacists can only add or change certain information as per Title 10, Part 80.

The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. This verification ensures the legitimacy of the prescription before dispensing.

All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear the name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient. If the CIN is missing, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the pharmacy records.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the importance of pharmacists verifying prescription details and consulting with prescribers when information is incomplete or requires clarification [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual provides specific guidance on handling missing patient information such as address, age, or CIN, allowing pharmacists to proceed if the information is readily available or the prescription is deemed legitimate, with specific rules for controlled substances [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy details the pharmacists responsibilities when prior authorization is required for non-preferred drugs, including notifying the prescriber and the patient, and the limited circumstances under which a pharmacist can initiate an emergency prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual explicitly states the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the prescriber's identity if the prescriber is unknown to them [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_38.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."

answers.headline_summary: When patient or prescriber information is missing, pharmacists are responsible for verifying the legitimacy of the prescription and the prescribers identity, especially if the prescriber is unknown. For missing patient details like address, age, or CIN, pharmacists can fill the prescription if the information is readily available in pharmacy records or if they are satisfied the prescription is legitimate.
answers.policy_definition:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber as necessary on any changes. This guidance is broader and does not specify actions for missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN.

When a non-preferred drug requires prior authorization (PA), the pharmacist must notify the prescriber that PA is required and inform the patient once the prescriber has obtained it. If the pharmacist cannot reach the prescriber and no PA is obtained, they may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only. Pharmacists are generally not authorized to submit for PAs or enter agreements to effectuate PAs on a prescribers behalf, except for emergency supplies or when employed by a prescriber.

If the address, age, or Client Identification Number (CIN) of a Medicaid member is missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter these items. This applies if the information is readily available in pharmacy records, the pharmacist knows the person requesting the fill, or the pharmacist is otherwise satisfied the prescription is legitimate. Prescriptions for controlled substances must still meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law.

The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the prescriber’s identity. This is particularly important if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order.

differences: The provider manual specifically addresses missing patient demographic information like address, age, or CIN, outlining conditions under which a pharmacist can still fill the prescription, whereas the policy update does not mention these specific scenarios [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The policy update details the pharmacist's responsibilities regarding prior authorization for non-preferred drugs, including notifying the prescriber and patient, and initiating emergency supplies, which is not covered in the provider manual's guidance on verifying prescriber identity [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

similarities: Both the provider manual and policy emphasize the pharmacists role in ensuring the legitimacy and accuracy of prescriptions [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].
differences: The provider manual specifies conditions under which pharmacists can proceed with filling a prescription despite missing patient information like address, age, or CIN, whereas the policy focuses on general verification of directions for use [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update September 2024 Volume 40 Number 10].

The provider manual addresses the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the identity of an unknown prescriber and the prescription's validity, while the policy details the process for pharmacists when dealing with non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

The provider manual addresses the pharmacist's responsibility to verify the identity of an unknown prescriber and the prescription's validity, while the policy details the process for pharmacists when dealing with non-preferred drugs requiring prior authorization [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12].

policy.text:
Reminder: Submitting for Prior Authorization of Non-Preferred Drugs
Prior authorization (PA) requests for non-preferred drugs must be submitted by the prescriber or by an
authorized agent of the prescriber. An authorized agent of the prescriber is considered an employee of the
prescribing practitioner and has access to the patient's medical records (i.e., nurse, medical assistant).
When presented with a prescription for a non-preferred drug that requires PA, the pharmacist must notify
the prescriber that PA is required and notify the patient once the prescriber has obtained the PA. If the
pharmacist is unable to reach the prescriber and a PA number has not yet been obtained by the prescriber,
the pharmacist may initiate a PA for a 72-hour emergency supply only.
Pharmacists are not authorized to submit for a PA nor enter into an agreement with a prescriber to
effectuate PAs and appeals on the prescriber’s behalf (except for the 72-hour emergency supply mentioned
above or when employed by a prescriber).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2023 Volume 39 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Providers should verify the prescription directions for use and consult the prescriber, as necessary, on any
changes.

policy.text:
Pharmacists may work with the patient and contact their prescriber to either change the
prescription to another drug or supply or obtain a prior authorization (PA) following the transition fill.
New York State Medicaid Update Special Edition – Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition pg. 4

Transition fills will be identified in the eMedNY claim response using NCPDP field 548-6F, Approved
Message Code, with code “005” — Claim paid under the plan's transition benefit period, otherwise claim
would have rejected as PA is required.
Vaccines Administered by Pharmacists
As indicated in the Scope of Benefits document, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/
redesign/mrt2/pharmacy_transition/repository/docs/rx_scope_of_benefits.pdf, vaccines that are administered
by pharmacists are subject to the benefit transition and should be billed to NYRx. Policy and billing guidance
for vaccines can be found within the New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacists as
Immunizers Fact Sheet, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/docs/phar
_immun_fact.pdf.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
If the address, age, or CIN of the Medicaid member are missing, the pharmacist is not required to enter any of these
items on the prescription if the information:

• Is otherwise readily available in the records of the pharmacy and the pharmacist knows the person who is
requesting that the prescription be filled, or
• The pharmacist is otherwise satisfied that the prescription is legitimate.
Prescriptions written for controlled substances must meet the requirements of Article 33 of the Public Health Law. In
accordance with New York State Department of Health Codes, Rules, and Regulations Title 10, Part 80, pharmacists are
permitted to add or change only certain information on controlled substance prescriptions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pharmacist is responsible for making a good faith effort to verify the validity of the prescription and the
prescriber’s identity if the prescriber is unknown to the pharmacist. Electronic imaging of prescriptions and fiscal
orders must result in an exact reproduction of the original order and may be required to be authenticated.
Storage Requirements – Vaccine and Medication
Medicaid Pharmacy providers are required to follow Regulations set forth by the Commissioner of Education,
section 63.6(b)(3) and the CDC guidelines in the CDC Storage and Handling Toolkit for vaccines and medication
requiring cold storage. Pharmacies must have a purpose-built or pharmaceutical grade refrigerator with adequate
storage space with fan-forced air circulation; a dorm-style or bar-style unit with an evaporator plate/cooling coil
does not qualify.

Additionally, Medicaid providers who are part of the Vaccine for Children (VFC) program must meet criteria for
refrigeration and vaccine storage set forth in the guidelines outlined in the Stand-Alone Storage Unit Purchasing
Guidance and Temperature Monitoring Requirements documents.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Required Prescribing Information
In accordance with NY State Education Law, all prescriptions written in New York State by a person authorized by New
York State to issue such prescriptions shall be transmitted electronically directly from prescriber to pharmacist in a
licensed pharmacy. Official New York State prescription forms or an oral prescription are accepted when exceptions
exist as noted in law.
All prescriptions and fiscal orders must bear:
• The name, address, age, and client identification number (CIN) of the patient for whom it is intended. If the
CIN does not appear on the order, the prescription should only be filled if the CIN is readily available in the
pharmacy records.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_40.ground_truth:
"A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.

The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice."
test_id_40.pm_ground_truth: A prescription must be complete and valid according to New York State Education Law and regulations. It places the ultimate responsibility on the pharmacist to ensure the legitimacy and completeness of the prescription before dispensing. The pharmacist must use their professional judgment.The manual permits the pharmacist to add certain missing patient information (like address, age, or gender) after verifying it with the patient or their agent. This is considered a standard exercise of professional practice.If critical information like drug strength, quantity, or directions is missing, the pharmacist must contact the prescriber to obtain the information. The pharmacist must then document this conversation, including the date, time, and the clarification received. Dispensing without this information is a violation of professional standards. If a claim is submitted with a non-enrolled or invalid prescriber NPI, the claim will be rejected. The manual's general guidance implies the pharmacy cannot fill the prescription for a Medicaid member until this is resolved.
test_id_40.policy_ground_truth: The Medicaid program's billing system assumes this professional responsibility is being met. If the pharmacist adds the patient's date of birth and it matches the Medicaid ID, the claim proceeds. The updates don't need to clarify this standard practice.

test_id_41.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_41.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, including those in compounded medications, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This exclusion applies to both fee-for-service and managed care programs.
answers.policy_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). The policy also prohibits compounds for cosmetic purposes.

Agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are excluded. This exclusion applies to both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) programs. This is additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3).
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). Compounded medications also cannot be made for cosmetic purposes or sexual dysfunction.

Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used in the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This applies to drugs listed on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

Both documents cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].
differences: The policy update explicitly mentions that the exclusion for weight loss drugs applies to both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) programs, which is not explicitly stated in the provider manual for this specific exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy update provides an additional state regulatory citation, NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3), for the exclusion of weight loss agents, which is not present in the provider manuals general statement on weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under New York State Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

Both documents cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that compounded medications cannot contain excluded drugs or be made for excluded indications, including weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

The policy update explicitly mentions that the exclusion for weight loss drugs applies to both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) programs, which is not explicitly stated in the provider manual for this specific exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy update provides an additional state regulatory citation, NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3), for the exclusion of weight loss agents, which is not present in the provider manuals general statement on weight loss drugs [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It does not include protocols that cover drugs
prescribed to relieve side effects of these therapies or to relieve distressing
symptoms (such as nausea or pain).

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
