The policy explicitly states that the exclusion for weight loss drugs applies to both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) programs, which is not explicitly mentioned in the provider manual for this specific exclusion [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy adds that the exclusion for weight loss drugs is additionally pursuant to New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3), providing further state regulatory context [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy mentions that submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other items utilized in the preparation or use of the final compounded product, a detail not present in the provider manuals discussion of compounded medications [New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update December 2020 Volume 36 Number 18
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
It does not include protocols that cover drugs
prescribed to relieve side effects of these therapies or to relieve distressing
symptoms (such as nausea or pain).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update January 2021 Volume 37 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Refills of compounds must be specifically requested by the patient or patient´s designee before the item is
prepared and submitted for payment.
• Ingredients in a compound claim will only be reimbursed if available on the NYS Medicaid List of Reimbursable
Drugs.
• Ingredients will only be reimbursed if they are compendia-supported for the intended route of administration
and indication.
• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or
for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYS Medicaid Fee-for-Service Programs.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to safety
reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or compendia-
supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_43.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_43.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, including those in compounded medications, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This exclusion applies to both fee-for-service and managed care programs.
answers.policy_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction, as outlined in the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

Agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are excluded for both Fee-for-Service (FFS) and Managed Care (MMC) programs. This exclusion is additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3).
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs used to treat weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

Medicaid does not reimburse for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is in accordance with the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot include drugs for weight loss, citing the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
differences: The policy update specifies that the exclusion of drugs for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain applies to both Fee-for-Service and Managed Care programs, a detail not explicitly mentioned in the provider manual for this specific point [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot include drugs for weight loss, citing the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both sources confirm that drugs for weight loss are an excluded benefit under NYS Medicaid [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy update specifies that the exclusion of drugs for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain applies to both Fee-for-Service and Managed Care programs, a detail not explicitly mentioned in the provider manual for this specific point [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

Note: Reimbursable drugs are listed alphabetically in sections by Rx Type.
Drug Coverage Limitations
Medicaid only provides reimbursement for drugs included on the New York State List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs
(unless provided by a facility which includes the cost of drugs in their all-inclusive rate). The following are examples of
drugs/drug uses which are not reimbursable by Medicaid in accordance with Policy and/or state or federal Legislation:
• Drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs for the treatment of sexual dysfunction pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2), and Social Services Law §365-a(4)(f).
• Drugs without a federal rebate agreement pursuant to SSA §1927(a).
• Drugs indicated for cosmetic use or hair growth pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(2).
• Drugs that are not approved as safe and effective by the FDA pursuant to SSA §1927(d)(4)(C).
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.

provider_manual.text:
Nothing in this policy is meant to suggest, encourage, or otherwise require that any practitioner
administered, or dispensed drug be billed to the Program under the pharmacy benefit. Practitioner administered drugs
will continue to be billed as a NYS Medicaid medical benefit.
Questions regarding this policy may be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy unit at (518)486-3209 or
NYRx@health.ny.gov.

test_id_45.question: Are weight loss drugs an excluded benefit?
test_id_45.concept: weight loss drugs excluded benefit status

answers.headline_summary: New York State Medicaid excludes coverage for drugs used for weight loss, including those in compounded medications, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2). This exclusion applies to both fee-for-service and managed care programs.
answers.policy_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications. This includes drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction, as per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

Agents used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain are excluded for both Fee-for-Service and Managed Care programs. This exclusion is additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18 §505.3(g)(3).
answers.provider_manual_definition:
Compounded medications are prohibited from containing excluded drugs or being made for indications excluded by NYS Medicaid. This includes, but is not limited to, drugs to treat weight loss, as specified by the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).

Medicaid does not provide reimbursement for drugs used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain. This exclusion is pursuant to the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2).
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot contain drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both documents cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
differences: The policy update specifies that the exclusion for drugs used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain applies to both Fee-for-Service and Managed Care programs, which is not explicitly stated in the provider manual for this specific point [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update explicitly state that compounded medications cannot contain drugs for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4].

Both documents cite the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) as the basis for excluding drugs used for weight loss [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4, New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

The policy update specifies that the exclusion for drugs used for anorexia, weight loss, or weight gain applies to both Fee-for-Service and Managed Care programs, which is not explicitly stated in the provider manual for this specific point [New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4].

policy.text:
This article serves as a reminder of those exclusions for both the FFS and MMC program areas, which are
as follows:
• agents when used for the treatment of anorexia, weight loss or weight gain [additionally pursuant to
New York Code, Rules and Regulations (NYCRR) Title 18 §505.3(g)(3)];
• agents when used for the treatment of sexual dysfunction unless such agents are used to treat a
condition, other than sexual or erectile dysfunction, for which the agents have been approved by the
Food and Drug Administration (FDA) [additionally pursuant to New York Social Services Law §365-
a(4)(f)]; and
• agents when used for cosmetic use or hair growth (additionally pursuant to NYCRR Title 18
§505.2(l)(5)).

policy.text:
April 2024 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 6

• Compounds may not contain excluded drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as
per the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title
19/1927.htm, including, but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual dysfunction or for
cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made to bypass the criteria within the NYRx, the Medicaid Pharmacy Program
Preferred Drug List, located at: https://newyork.fhsc.com/downloads/providers/NYRx_PDP_PDL.pdf.
• Compounds may not be made with or to replace drug products removed from the marketplace due to
safety reasons.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation or use of final compounded product.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2024 Volume 40 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
December 2020 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 9

• Compounds may not contain drugs or be made for NYS Medicaid excluded indications as per the
Social Security Act §1927(d)(2) including but not limited to drugs to treat weight loss or sexual
dysfunction or for cosmetic purposes.
• Compounds may not be made in therapeutic amounts or combinations not FDA-approved, or
compendia-supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes or other non-active ingredient
additives to a commercially available product.
• Submitted compound claims may not include packaging materials or containers, syringes, or other
items utilized or necessary in the preparation of final compounded product.
• Compounds must be adjudicated with the appropriate and matching final compounded product route
code for the dosage form.
• Prepared compounds that mimic a commercial product must include on the prescription and in the
members medical chart documentation of the reason for compounding (i.e., sensitivity or
contraindication to dyes, preservatives, or fillers or lack of availability of a commercial product).

reasons.

supported for use.
• Compounds may not be made to add coloring, flavoring, perfumes, or other non-active ingredient additives to
a commercially available product.

test_id_46.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their 340B acquisition cost, with a professional dispensing fee. New York State Medicaid implements system edits to ensure the ingredient cost submitted for 340B drugs does not exceed the 340B ceiling price defined by HRSA.
answers.policy_definition:
For New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service primary claims, system editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price. This ensures that the submitted cost does not exceed the maximum allowed amount. Claims that exceed the ceiling price will be denied with specific error codes.

The 340B ceiling price represents the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for a 340B-covered outpatient drug, as defined by HRSA. New York State Medicaid uses this ceiling price to validate the ingredient costs submitted for 340B drugs.

340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be properly identified as 340B for both Fee-for-Service and Medicaid Managed Care members. These claims must also be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice for Fee-for-Service members, net of any manufacturer discounts.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drugs, reimbursement is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is also included when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim.

The 340B ceiling price is defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the Unit Rebate Amount (URA), as determined by HRSA. Actual Acquisition Cost refers to the price paid by the provider or covered agency for a 340B purchased drug, without additional costs.

New York Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to prevent duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove them from rebate invoicing.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update state that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both documents emphasize the importance of the 340B ceiling price, as defined by HRSA, in the reimbursement process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources highlight the necessity of proper identification for 340B drug claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual specifies a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for eligible 340B purchased drug claims, which is not explicitly detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update describes system edits that compare the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price for Fee-for-Service primary claims, a detail not present in the provider manuals general reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The provider manual states that New York Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File, relying instead on claim-level identifiers, while the policy update focuses on the required NCPDP fields for claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update state that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both documents emphasize the importance of the 340B ceiling price, as defined by HRSA, in the reimbursement process [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources highlight the necessity of proper identification for 340B drug claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

The provider manual specifies a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for eligible 340B purchased drug claims, which is not explicitly detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update describes system edits that compare the submitted ingredient cost with the 340B ceiling price for Fee-for-Service primary claims, a detail not present in the provider manuals general reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The provider manual states that New York Medicaid does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File, relying instead on claim-level identifiers, while the policy update focuses on the required NCPDP fields for claim submission [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.

test_id_46.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."

It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_47.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their 340B acquisition cost, with a professional dispensing fee. New York State Medicaid implements system edits to ensure the ingredient cost submitted for 340B drugs does not exceed the 340B ceiling price.
answers.policy_definition:
For New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service primary claims, system editing compares the submitted ingredient cost with the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)-defined 340B ceiling price. This ensures the ingredient cost does not exceed the maximum allowed, and claims exceeding this ceiling price will be denied.

340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service and Medicaid managed care members. These claims must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice for fee-for-service members, net any manufacturer discounts.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
For 340B purchased drugs, reimbursement is based on the 340B Acquisition Cost. A professional dispensing fee of $10.18 is also applied when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim.

New York Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove them from rebate invoicing. The New York Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources highlight the importance of identifying 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual specifies a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update describes a system edit that compares the submitted ingredient cost to the HRSA-defined 340B ceiling price, a detail not explicitly mentioned in the provider manuals reimbursement methodology section [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The provider manual explicitly states that the NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File, relying instead on claim-level identifiers, while the policy update focuses on the requirement for proper identification of 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update emphasize that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

Both sources highlight the importance of identifying 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

The provider manual specifies a professional dispensing fee of $10.18 for 340B purchased drugs, which is not detailed in the policy update [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The policy update describes a system edit that compares the submitted ingredient cost to the HRSA-defined 340B ceiling price, a detail not explicitly mentioned in the provider manuals reimbursement methodology section [New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The provider manual explicitly states that the NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File, relying instead on claim-level identifiers, while the policy update focuses on the requirement for proper identification of 340B claims [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Pharmacy
Reminder: 340B Providers, Covered Entities, and Contract Pharmacies
A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and
Customary (U&C). 340B drug claims need to be properly identified as 340B as well as submitted at costs in the
appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.
The following fields are required on each Medicaid 340B drug claim submitted NCPDP format:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)

423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08 Not applicable
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted * 340B Acquisition
Not applicable
Cost

to Cash Customers Customers

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_48.ground_truth:
"Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.

drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity."
test_id_48.pm_ground_truth: Pharmacies that dispense 340B-purchased drugs to Medicaid members must have a mechanism to prevent duplicate discounts. It states that these claims must be identified at the point of sale. The provider manual does not specify the reimbursement rate for 340B drugs. It describes reimbursement formulas for drugs in general (e.g., based on Average Acquisition Cost - AAC) but is silent on the specific rate for drugs purchased under the 340B program.
test_id_48.policy_ground_truth: drugs purchased through the 340B program are reimbursed at the 340B Acquisition Cost. This is a direct conflict with any assumption that 340B drugs would be paid using the standard generic or brand formulas. It establishes that the reimbursement is tied directly to the discounted price paid by the covered entity.

test_id_49.question: How are the 340B drugs reimbursed?

answers.headline_summary: 340B drugs are reimbursed based on their 340B acquisition cost, with a professional dispensing fee. The ingredient cost submitted for 340B drugs must not exceed the 340B ceiling price, as defined by HRSA.
answers.policy_definition:
For NYS Medicaid FFS primary claims, system editing compares the submitted ingredient cost (NCPDP field 409-DK) with the 340B ceiling price. This comparison ensures the submitted cost does not exceed the maximum allowed. Claims with an ingredient cost higher than the ceiling price will be denied.

The 340B ceiling price is defined by HRSA as the maximum amount a manufacturer can charge a covered entity for a 340B-covered outpatient drug. The ingredient cost submitted should not be higher than this price.

340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP D.0 format must be properly identified as 340B for both FFS and MMC members. These claims must be submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice for FFS members, net any manufacturer discounts.
answers.provider_manual_definition:
340B purchased drugs are reimbursed at their 340B Acquisition Cost. This reimbursement includes a $10.18 professional dispensing fee when the claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim.

The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). Actual Acquisition Cost refers to the price paid for a drug by the provider or covered agency for a 340B purchased drug, with no added costs.

NY Medicaid relies on 340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required for all 340B drug claims to remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
similarities: Both the provider manual and policy update indicate that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost and must not exceed the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources emphasize the importance of identifying 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
differences: The provider manual specifies a $10.18 professional dispensing fee in addition to the 340B Acquisition Cost, which is not explicitly detailed in the policy updates reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides a detailed definition of Actual Acquisition Cost, whereas the policy update focuses more on the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The policy update mentions specific NCPDP fields required for 340B drug claims, such as Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, which are not detailed in the provider manuals general statement about claim level identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].
comparison: Both the provider manual and policy update indicate that 340B drug reimbursement is tied to the 340B acquisition cost and must not exceed the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

Both sources emphasize the importance of identifying 340B claims to prevent duplicate discounts [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

The provider manual specifies a $10.18 professional dispensing fee in addition to the 340B Acquisition Cost, which is not explicitly detailed in the policy updates reimbursement methodology [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines].

The provider manual provides a detailed definition of Actual Acquisition Cost, whereas the policy update focuses more on the 340B ceiling price [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines, New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11].

The policy update mentions specific NCPDP fields required for 340B drug claims, such as Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 and Basis of Cost Determination (BCD) 08, which are not detailed in the provider manuals general statement about claim level identifiers [New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9].

policy.text:
National Drug Code Reporting Requirements
Providers are required to report the National Drug Code (NDC) for all PADs billed by a private practitioner to
NYS Medicaid and for all ordered ambulatory claims billed by clinics. If the NDC is not referenced on a claim,
NYS Medicaid will not provide reimbursement for the drug.
Drugs obtained via the 340B program must be identified by appending the UD modifier to the 340B drug line
on submitted NYS Medicaid FFS-only claims. Providers are required to report the NDC on FFS claims for the
reimbursement of 340B drugs. Providers must also include the number of units administered and their actual
acquisition cost, by invoice, in the charges field of a claim. Please note: Medicare requires either the JG or
TB modifiers be appended to the 340B drug line for Medicare/NYS Medicaid crossover claims (no UD modifier
is needed on crossover claims).
An accurate NDC must be reported for all PADs billed to NYS Medicaid FFS on an institutional claim that
uses Ambulatory Patient Groups (APGs) payment methodology.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2022 Volume 38 Number 8

A review of 340B claims submitted via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format
indicates confusion as to which field should contain the ingredient cost and which should contain the Usual and

appropriate fields. Claims submitted with incorrect data, including cost in the wrong field, are subject to
audit and recovery.

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2019 Volume 35 Number 6
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the
Ingredient Cost for 340B Drugs
As previously communicated in the January 2018 and December 2018 Medicaid Update publications, 340B drug
claims submitted to Medicaid via the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0 format are:
• required to be properly identified as 340B for both fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid managed care
(MMC) members; as well as
• submitted at the 340B acquisition cost by invoice to the provider for FFS members, net any manufacturer
discounts and/or other price reductions.
The following fields are required on Medicaid 340B drug claims submitted via NCPDP:
Medicaid Primary Medicaid Secondary Claim
Field
Claim (Primary: Medicare; Commercial)
420-DK, Submission Clarification Code (SCC) 20 20
423-DN, Basis of Cost Determination (BCD)* 08 No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
409-D9, Ingredient Cost Submitted* 340B Acquisition No Requirements Specific to Medicaid
Cost
426-DQ, Usual and Customary Cost (U&C) ** Lowest Net Charge Lowest Net Charge to Cash Customers
to Cash Customers
*MMC plans should be consulted on their requirements for this field.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2019 Volume 35 Number 9
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Outpatient Dispensed Drugs (NCPDP)
As previously communicated in the New System Edit to be Implemented to Validate the Ingredient Cost for
340B Drugs article published in the August 2019 issue of the Medicaid Update, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2019/aug19_mu.pdf, for a NYS Medicaid
FFS primary claim only, system editing will compare the ingredient cost submitted (NCPDP field 409-DK)
with the 340B ceiling price for the product, as defined by the Health Resources and Services Administration
(HRSA), to ensure the ingredient cost submitted is the 340B price. The 340B ceiling price refers to the
maximum amount that a manufacturer can charge the CE for the purchase of a 340B-covered outpatient drug.
A claim submitted to NYS Medicaid should never be higher than the 340B ceiling price. Pharmacies that
submit a 340B drug claim, whenever the ingredient cost submitted is higher than the ceiling price, will be
returned the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) Rx Denial code: “708: Exceeds NY Allowed
Maximum”, and the NCPDP Reject code: "78: Cost Exceeds Maximum”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The pricing methodology is systematically determined as follows:
Drug Type If NADAC is available If NADAC is unavailable, Professional Dispensing Fee*
reimburse at: reimburse at:
Lower of NADAC, FUL, Lower of WAC – 17.5%,
Generics SMAC or U&C FUL, SMAC, or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC or
Brands U&C Lower of WAC or U&C $10.18
Lower of NADAC, FUL,
OTCs SMAC or U&C Lower of WAC, FUL, $10.18
SMAC, or U&C
340B**Purchased 340B Acquisition Cost 340B Acquisition Cost $10.18
Drug
Note: Claims will pay at the pharmacy's Usual and Customary (U&C) pricing if lower than drug ingredient cost plus
dispensing fee.
* Professional Dispensing Fee applies if the drug meets the definition of Covered Outpatient Drug and is not paid at
U&C.
**When claim is eligible for submission as a 340B purchased drug claim. See subsection 340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
below for more information.
Federal Upper Limit (FUL) is determined by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a price ceiling used by Centers
for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) to control prices for certain medications paid to pharmacies.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
6.0 Definitions
The following terms are defined for the purposes of the NY Medicaid program and are included to help clarify policies
as provided in this Manual:
340B Ceiling Price
The 340B ceiling price is statutorily defined as the Average Manufacturer Price (AMP) reduced by the rebate
percentage, which is commonly referred to as the Unit Rebate Amount (URA). HRSA obtains the AMP and URA data
from the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) as part of quarterly reporting for the Medicaid Drug
Rebate Program. This figure is then multiplied by the package size and case package size to produce a price that is
used in the marketplace for purchasing covered outpatient drugs. For example, the AMP minus the URA indicates the
cost of one pill.
Actual Acquisition Cost
Refers to the price paid for a drug or supply by the provider or in the case of a Covered Agency for a 340B purchased
drug, from a supplier with no added costs, delivery fees or dispensing fees, or means the agency's determination of the
pharmacy providers' actual prices paid to acquire drug products marketed or sold by specific manufacturers.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
340B Pharmacy Drug Claims
Upon enrollment in the 340B program, 340B covered entities must determine whether they will use 340B drugs for
their Medicaid patients.
Federal law prohibits duplicate discounts – manufacturers are not required to provide a discounted 340B price and a
Medicaid drug rebate for the same drug. The federal Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) has rules
and requirements for when a covered entity uses 340B drugs for Medicaid members, including listing the entity’s
Medicaid provider number/NPI on the HRSA Medicaid Exclusion File (MEF). Information on HRSA’s requirements in this
area can be found at the following site: http://www.hrsa.gov/opa/programrequirements/medicaidexclusion/index.html.
The NY Medicaid program does not use HRSA’s Medicaid Exclusion File. NY Medicaid relies completely on the use of
340B claim level identifiers to avoid duplicate discounts. These identifiers are required at the claim submission level for
all 340B drug claims, thereby avoiding duplicate discounts. Using these identifiers is the only way NY Medicaid will
remove the claim from rebate invoicing.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
All prescription drugs are reimbursed on a fee-for-service basis. Residential health care
facilities may:
• Operate an institutional pharmacy to provide these items or
• Contract with Medicaid enrolled pharmacies to provide these items to Medicaid members. The pharmacy
must be reimbursed by the facility for all non-prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Prescription
drugs may be billed directly to Medicaid by the dispensing pharmacy on a fee-for-service basis.
Drugs billed directly to Medicaid are limited to prescription drugs included on the New York State Medicaid List of
Reimbursable Drugs: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx and are subject to refill, frequency, quantity and
duration, step therapy, and prior authorization/approval requirements as described in this Manual.

Medicaid members with both Medicare and Medicaid (dual eligible Medicaid members) who have met their residency
requirements in a residential health care facility will receive their prescription drug coverage from their Medicare Part D
Plan.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health
